content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sncramcnlo, CA 9581 t 
( 9 I 6) :i 2 2 -5 6 6 0 • Fa X ( 9 I 6) 3 2 2 -0 8 8 6 
Ariel Hurtado, MD 
I 871 Severinsen Street 
Hollister, CA 95023 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-100 
Dear Mr. Hurtado: 
September I 2, 201 7 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions 
under the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1 and Section 1090. Please note that we do not advise on 
any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are 
also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 }, and any 
advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law 
conflict of interest. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Benito County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1 (c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section I 097 .1 ( c ){ 5}.) 
QUESTION 
As a member of the San Benito Health Care District Board, may you participate in a 
decision before the Board concerning the selection of a health insurance plan for hospital 
employees when as a doctor you see patients that have the insurance plan currently in place for 
hospital employees? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
FileNo. A-17-100 
Page No. 2 
Yes, you may participate in the decision because you do not have a financial interest under 
Section I 090 and there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon your financial 
interests under the Act. 
FACTS 
You are a member of the Board of Directors ("Board") for the San Benito Health Care 
District ("District") and represent District 3. This position is designated in your agency's conflict of 
interest code. You are also a practicing anesthesiologist. You perform anesthesia for all surgeries 
performed at Hazel Hawkins Hospital ("hospital") which is run by the District. The anesthesiology 
services are contracted to Dr. Amit Mathur who runs them through his corporation ACE 
Anesthesia. 
You are not involved with the management or financials of ACE Anesthesia in any way. 
Dr. Mathur negotiates his contract directly with the District. You are employed as an independent 
subcontractor through your own S corporation. Dr. Mathur pays all of the anesthesiologists based 
on the shift type and the amount of time spent performing cases. Every 15 minutes is considered 
one unit and each person is reimbursed at the same rate. This is called a blended unit, meaning that 
regardless of insurance or patient's ability to pay all anesthesiologists are paid the same rate for the 
same work. You pay your own malpractice insurance and professional fees. 
You also work one day a week as an independent pain doctor in the community. You 
perform this work under another corporation that you set up called Warrior Wellness, lnc. You only 
see referrals from other doctors in the community who require some type of interventional 
procedure. Your practice sees patients with all types of insurance plans. You accept all insurance 
plans for this work and do not discriminate based on insurance the patient has. 
The District is negotiating an insurance plan for the hospital employees. They currently have 
a plan run by the San Benito Medical Associates (SBMA) which is administered through the Pacific 
Health Alliance. The SBMA is a group of doctors in San Benito County which has come together to 
negotiate .better rates for their members and promote their organization. You are not a part of their 
organization. The Pacific Health Alliance handles the administration and pays the claims for 
SBMA. 
Your medical biller has informed you that less than I 0% of your pain management patients 
are hospital employees with Pacific Health Alliance insurance. 
There is a possibility that a vote may occur before the District Board asking for a 
termination or a renewal of the SBMA contract. The District is self-insured and pays a set monthly 
amount to SBMA to run the plan. The rates you are paid for professional services are determined by 
the plan and you have no way of knowing what they might be if the District changes plans. Despite 
a change in plans you will still be seeing the same number of hospital employees as before. 


ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 
FileNo. A-17-100 
Page No. 3 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
firnmcial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. City ofTaft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited 
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. 
Honig (1996} 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section I 090 is void, regardless of 
whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 
Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) When Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a 
public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the 
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.) 
Based upon the facts provided, the determinative issue is whether you have a financial 
interest in the contract. Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which 
the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333.) Although Section 
1090 does not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General 
opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve 
financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (People v. 
Vallerga (1977} 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fu. 5; Teny v. Bender ( 1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-
208; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).) 
"[T]he term 'financially interested' in section 1090 cannot be interpreted in a restricted and 
technical manner." (People v. Honig, supra, 48 Cal.App.4th at p. 315.) The defining characteristic 
of a prohibited financial interest is whether it has the potential to divide an official's loyalties and 
compromise the undivided representation of the public interests the official is charged with 
protecting. (See Stigall v. City of Taft, supra, 58 Cal.2d at p. 569.) Thus, that the interest "might be 
small or indirect is immaterial so long as it is such as deprives the [people] of his overriding fidelity 
· to [them] and places him in the compromising situation' where, in the exercise of his official 
judgment or discretion, he may be influenced by personal considerations rather than the public 
good." (Teny v. Bender (1956) 143 Cal. App. 2d 198, 208 [300 P.2d 119]; see also Thomson v. 
Call, supra, 38 Cal.3d at p. 645 [direct and indirect interests are equally prohibited].)) Put in 
ordinary, but nonetheless precise, terms, an official has a financial interest in a contract if he might 
profit from it. (People v. Honigt supra, 48 Cal.App.4th at p. 338.) Anotper court has opined that 
" ... if the contract itself offers no benefit to the official, either directly or indirectly, then the official 
is not financially interested in the contract and any explicit legislative exemption for such a 
circumstance would be unnecessarily redundant. (Eden Township Healthcare Dist. v. Sutter 
Health, (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 208.) 
While you do receive payments from the insurance provider that the hospital currently uses, 
it is your patients that select the provider and the patients that select you in order for you to receive 
those payments. You accept all types of insurance and do not discriminate against patients based 
upon the insurance provider they have. Thus, it is the patients who determine whether you will be 


FileNo. A-17-100 
Page No. 4 
paid by an insurance company and not yourself Therefore, there is no potential financial gain to 
you or possibility of profit as result of the current decision before the District because it will have 
no bearing on the patients you see or profit you receive, thus no financial interest under Section 
1090. 
The Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state 
or local government agency. As a member of the Board, you are a public official. 
Financial interests from which conflicts of interests may arise are defined in Section 87103 
as follows: 
• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)) or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103( d).) 
., 
• 
An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or 
more. (Section 87103(b).) An indirect interest in business or real property means a business 
or real property interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of the official. 
• An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
"Income" is defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse 
and a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official ( or his 
or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a JO-percent or greater interest. 
(Section 82030(a).) 
• An interest in a source of gifts to the official if the gifts aggregate to $470 or more within 12 
months prior to the decision. (Sectioq 87103(e).) 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.) 
The specific financial interests2 relevant to your inquiry are: 
I. Business Entity: You have a financial interest in your S Corporation and Warrior 
Wellness, Inc. (Section 87103(a).) 
2. Sources of Income: Your S Corporation and Warrior Wellness, Inc. are also sources of 
income to you. In addition, your patients and any doctors from whom you receive income, such as 
2 A public official also has a financial interest in his or her personal finances. However, there no facts to 
suggest your personal finances would be affected as a result of this decision. 


FileNo. A-17-100 
Page No. 5 
Dr. Marthur would be considered a source of income to you to the extent that their income to you or 
your businesses is $500 or more.3 (Section 87103(c).) 
Under most circumstances, the Pacific Health Alliance and SBMA would be a source of 
income to you because of the payments you receive on behalf of your patients. However, the 
Commission has advised that if a patient exercises sufficient control over payments to a doctor by 
selecting that doctor, instead of other doctors, the patient, rather than their insurance carrier, is the 
source of income to the doctor. (Morris Advice Letter, No. l-90-373.) You indicate that you see 
patients that have Pacific Health Alliance insurance, but you also accept patients who have all other 
insurances and do not discriminate against patients based on insurance carrier. Again, as mentioned 
under the Section 1090 analysis above, these patients select you as their provider and their 
insurance makes payments to you on their behalf. Under these circumstances, Pacific Health 
Alliance and SBMA are not a source of income to you, as the income otherwise provided is paid on 
behalf of the covered beneficiary by contractual arrangements. Thus, each patient who selects you 
as his or her medical provider is a potential source of income to you, and not the insurance provider. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Under the Act, an effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision, and an interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, 
or subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) In this case, your businesses and sources of 
income are not a named party in or subject of the decision, therefore these interests are not 
explicitly involved. 
Regulation 18702.1 (b) sets forth the standard for materiality applicable to the effect of a 
decision on an official's interest in a business entity that is not explicitly involved in a decision. 
"(b) For a governmental decision not identified in subdivision (a), the financial 
effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a 
change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a 
privately-held business entity." 
There is no indication that there will be a change in the value of your business 
interests as a result of the decision before the Board, therefore there is no conflict with 
respect to those businesses. 
Regulation 18702.3(a)(4) provides that if the source of income is a business entity, 
materiality is determined pursuant to the standard for materiality for the effect of a decision on an 
official's interest in a business entity contained in Regulation 18702.1. None of the standards 
provided in Regulation 18702.1 are applicable to your S Corporation or Warrior Wellness, Inc. 
Regulation 18702.3(a)(2) provides the materiality standards for individuals who are sources 
of income not explicitly involved in the decision. Under this regulation, an effect on an individual is 
1 Under Section 82030(a), if a public official owns a I O•percent or greater interest in a business, a source of 
income to that business is also a source of income to the official. 


FileNo. A-17-100 
Page No. 6 
material if the individual will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision, or if 
the official knows or has reason to know that the individual has an interest in a business entity or 
real property that will be financially affected. However, there is no indication that any patient or 
doctor, who is a source of income to you, will be materially affected by the decision. Therefore, you 
do not have a conflict of interest in the decision under the Act. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
SKB:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack Woodside 
General Counsel 
By; 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

paid by an insurance company and not yourself Therefore, there is no potential financial gain to
